image0.name: step_4_mse_comparison_1_1776466132.png
image0.caption: Comparison of the final position Mean Squared Error (MSE) between the Hamiltonian Neural Network (HNN) model and a point-mass baseline for simulations with N=25, 50, and 100 particles. The HNN model was trained exclusively on N=50 systems, thus the results for N=25 and N=100 demonstrate its zero-shot transferability. The model maintains a consistently low error across all particle counts, outperforming the baseline by several orders of magnitude and showing robust generalization to system sizes not seen during training.

image1.name: step_4_hamiltonian_deviation_2_1776466132.png
image1.caption: The deviation of the total energy (Hamiltonian) from its initial value, $H(t) - H(0)$, for five distinct test simulations integrated over a time of $T=5.0$. The energy error exhibits bounded oscillations without any secular drift, providing empirical evidence that the symplectic Hamiltonian Neural Network with its embedded leapfrog integrator has learned a conservative system. This behavior prevents the unphysical injection or dissipation of energy, which is a critical feature for the stable long-term emulation of N-body dynamics.
